Categories
The controlling doctrines and exceptions of street-level constitutional law, each anchored to its leading case. State law can be more protective than these federal floors — in Texas, Art. 38.23 often is. Doctrine summaries should be verified against the anchor opinions before publication.
Code Lookup
State
Texas is live. Additional states are in development and will appear here as they're verified.
Keyword search
Code
Categories
Summaries and offense levels are drafted for field reference and must be verified against the current statutes at statutes.capitol.texas.gov before publication — the Legislature amends these codes every session. Each section includes a verbatim-text dropdown: entries marked pending have not yet been populated from the official source, and only source-verified text will ever appear there.
Arrest Authority
State
Synced with the State Codes tab — switch jurisdiction there and this tab updates to match. Warrantless arrest authority is state-specific; Texas is live, other states will be added with their own statutory frameworks.
Keyword search
Your scenario
Two locks on every warrantless arrest in Texas. An arrest must be authorized by a specific statutory exception (CCP Chapter 14 or another statute) and supported by Fourth Amendment probable cause. Texas law is more restrictive than the federal floor — and under Art. 38.23, evidence from an arrest that violates the statute can be suppressed even when the Constitution was satisfied.
Ordinance Lookup
In Development
Jurisdiction
Local ordinances are jurisdiction-specific. This is a selected set of enforcement-relevant chapters, not the complete Frisco Code of Ordinances.
Keyword search
Ordinance category
Local ordinances supplement, not replace, state law. A city ordinance cannot authorize conduct state law forbids, and most Class C ordinance violations are fine-only with no custodial arrest unless another statutory hook applies (e.g., failure to identify, outstanding warrant). Always confirm an ordinance is currently in force — cities amend their codes routinely — before relying on it.
Starred items and your most recently viewed entries (via Search Everything), stored only on this device — nothing here is uploaded or synced.
Disclaimer. This resource is provided free of charge to individual peace officers for educational purposes only. It is not legal advice, does not create an attorney–client relationship, and is not a substitute for your agency's policies, your legal advisor, or the current text of controlling authority. Case law and statutes change; always verify a holding or section is current before relying on it. Officers are responsible for acting within their agency policy and the law of their jurisdiction.
